html:
Gustav IV Adolf, born on November 1, 1778, at Stockholm Castle, and died on February 7, 1837, in St. Gallen, was King of Sweden from 1792 to 1809. He was the son of Gustav III and Sophia Magdalena, and married to Frederica of Baden. During the Napoleonic Wars, he consistently opposed Napoleon I, which ultimately led to the Finnish War, resulting in the loss of the entire region of Finland and the province of Österbotten. The dissatisfaction with his rule was so strong that he was overthrown in a coup d'état in 1809, and he spent the rest of his life on the continent under increasingly impoverished and alcoholic conditions.

== Biography ==

=== Childhood ===

Gustav IV Adolf was born at Stockholm Castle on November 1, 1778, as the son of Gustav III and Sofia Magdalena. All residents of Stockholm had daily free access to see the newborn crown prince. The prince's lady-in-waiting, Hedvig Sofia von Rosen, complained to Gustav III about "the impropriety of women undressing and tending to the child in the presence of so many people, who would stare and often need comforting." However, the king replied that the crown prince should get used to being seen by the public. Due to fears of a smallpox epidemic, this "public reception" for the crown prince was discontinued after a few days, but it resumed after his baptism. In May 1783, the crown prince was vaccinated against smallpox. This method was still new in Europe and not without risks, but following a recommendation from Empress Catherine II (who was also his cousin), Gustav III agreed to the vaccination.

During the public celebrations at Gustav Adolf's Square (then called Norrmalmstorget) that marked the baptism, a tragedy occurred. The square was overcrowded with enthusiastic celebrants, and panic broke out when 60 to 100 people were trampled to death.

On November 10 of the same year, 19 nobles, 10 priests, 10 citizens, and 10 peasants were appointed as godparents to the prince. All the godparents received Gustav III's godfather insignia in a ceremony on December 27, 1778. Among his godparents were Magnus Fredrik Brahe and Nils Svensson from Håslöv.

Early on, malicious rumors spread that Gustav III was not the father of the child, but rather the nobleman Adolf Fredrik Munck from eastern Finland. He had assisted the royal couple during their sexual debut. Despite the royal couple appearing to have a happy marriage at the time of the queen's first pregnancy, the rumor persisted, even being spread by Gustav III's brother, Duke Carl, who then told their mother, Lovisa Ulrika, leading to a rift between the king and his mother, which was not resolved until her deathbed. The rumor was so widespread that it is recorded in "Svenska adelns Ättar-taflor" that Count Munck af Fulkila is believed to have secretly married Queen Sofia Magdalena, and "is presumed to be the father of Gustav IV Adolf."

The king was deeply involved in his elder son's upbringing, and the prince was separated from his mother in 1780 to begin his education with male tutors and attendants. This was a departure from the norm, as this typically occurred between the ages of three and five. The emotional separation caused conflict between the king and Sofia Magdalena, despite her persistent attempts to persuade him to change his mind. According to Axel von Fersen the elder, the prince was "frivolous and vain" and spoiled by his mother and her ladies-in-waiting. However, Gustav III got his way, and Gustav Adolf began his education as a prince. In July 1781, the prince was assigned his own tutor, the pedantic councilor Fredrik Sparre, who would be primarily responsible for his upbringing and education. It soon became apparent that the crown prince's greatest pleasure "was military exercises from morning to evening," as Sparre wrote in his diary: "Playing soldier, grenadier, sentry, officer, standing guard, exercising, marching, and drumming are activities that the Prince is endlessly enthusiastic about, every day for more than three weeks."

In November 1784, the young prince met his new tutor, Nils von Rosenstein. Under von Rosenstein, Gustav Adolf learned French, Latin, history, geography, and philosophy. According to von Rosenstein, the prince made great progress in his studies and mastered French at the age of eight, as well as some Latin. To encourage his son to use the Swedish language, the king hired secretary Carlström to teach the prince how to write in Swedish.

When the Russo-Swedish War broke out, the ten-year-old crown prince followed the events with great interest, and when news arrived of the Battle of Hogland, he cried tears of joy that a Russian frigate had been captured.

At the age of thirteen, Gustav Adolf experienced the murder of his father, a trauma that left deep scars. He broke down in solitary weeping, but publicly he appeared calm and composed. The events affected him so much that for a time he saw no other way out than to renounce the crown. However, he was persuaded to change his mind by Gyldenstolpe. Hedvig Charlotta described him at this time as follows:

The emotional turmoil he experienced took its toll on him.

How certain can we be about this?

Information about people from the 19th century is very well documented (through census records, newspapers, and letters). The uncertainty mainly concerns the selection of information and the perspective – whose stories are being highlighted.
tldr: Gustav IV Adolph was a Swedish king who ruled the country between 1792 and 1809. He lost significant portions of Sweden's territory in wars against Napoleon, and he was eventually deposed from his throne.
lattlast:
Gustav IV Adolf was born at Stockholm Castle in 1778. As a child, he received a great deal of public attention, but there was a tragic accident during a celebration where many people died due to being crushed in a square. He also received the smallpox vaccination, which was a new and risky procedure at the time.

As he grew up, he focused heavily on military training and learned several languages, including French and Latin. After his father, King Gustav III, was assassinated when the prince was thirteen years old, he experienced a significant shock that affected him for many years.

During his reign as king, he chose to fight against Napoleon, which led to war with Russia. This resulted in Sweden losing Finland and large parts of its territory. Due to widespread discontent among the people, he was deposed from the throne in 1809 and moved abroad, where he lived out the rest of his life.
